title: Hormuz Reopens, but Nothing Returns to Normal
subhead: Ships trickle through the Strait of Hormuz as mine-clearing drags on, Russia imports gasoline from India to cover wartime refinery losses, and the UK seizes a Russian shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel — another day where energy flows are being redrawn by force.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Slow Reopening, Permanent Changes
sections.body:
Ships have started moving through the Strait of Hormuz again after the US-Iran memorandum of understanding signed June 17, but full normalization is weeks away. Iran's Fars news agency reported that three oil tankers and two cargo ships transited the strait in the first days after the deal. US President Trump declared the waterway would be 'completely open' by the signing ceremony, scheduled for July 4 in Switzerland, according to the Saudi Gazette. About 500 ships with 20,000 crew remain backed up waiting to pass, according to the International Chamber of Shipping.

Mine-clearing operations could take 40 to 50 days before insurers and shipping companies are confident enough to resume normal traffic, Reuters reported. France and the UK will coordinate a mine-clearing and reopening mission, French President Macron announced alongside Trump.

More consequentially, Iran is not returning the strait to its pre-war status. Under Article 5 of the MoU, passage is toll-free for only 60 days. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei said Tehran will charge 'maritime service fees' — not tolls, a distinction Oman rejects. Iran also published a map in May claiming regulatory control over a stretch of the strait extending into UAE and Omani territorial waters, prompting five Gulf states to formally warn shipping companies through the International Maritime Organisation. The Hindu reports that Iran will conduct talks with Oman 'to define the future administration and maritime services in the Strait of Hormuz' — language that reasserts Iranian control rather than restoring the pre-war freedom of navigation.

Rystad Energy chief economist Claudio Galimberti warned that 'sentiment is not the same as supply.' Retaliatory Iranian strikes damaged oil and gas infrastructure across several Gulf states, and ramping production back up will take time. 'It's not just a tap you turn back on,' said Philippe Chalmin of the Cyclope group.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait is reopening, but the terms are new: Iran is asserting regulatory control and charging fees. The 60-day toll-free window expires in mid-August. What happens after that will define Gulf energy logistics for years.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Return to Pre-War Levels — Tanker Data Tells a Different Story
sections.body:
Brent crude traded near $72-73/barrel at the start of July, roughly back to pre-war levels — down from a peak near $130 during the height of the Hormuz closure. Citi cut its Q3 2026 Brent forecast to $75 and Q4 to $70. Morgan Stanley lowered its Brent price view for the rest of 2026 and 2027, citing faster-than-expected Hormuz reopening and a looming supply surplus. Fitch Ratings estimates Brent will average $100-110/bbl in May-July before falling to $80 in August and $70 from September.

But a Reuters Breakingviews analysis published July 1 argues that tanker data is a better gauge than oil prices. The sugar rush from clearing stuck cargoes masks an underlying supply deficit: freight rates for Hormuz-transiting vessels remain elevated, and actual throughput has not recovered. Analysts at SPI Asset Management note the industry is shifting from 'just-in-time' to 'just-in-case' logistics — meaning more storage, more flexible shipping, and higher baseline costs.

Meanwhile, the UAE's decision to quit OPEC effective May 1 continues to ripple. With the UAE free to pump at capacity and Iraq signaling it may also leave the cartel unless quotas are raised, OPEC's ability to manage prices is eroding at exactly the moment the market needs coordination.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil prices look normal, but the physical market tells a different story. Elevated freight, damaged Gulf infrastructure, and OPEC fragmentation mean the return to 'normal' prices masks ongoing disruption underneath.

sections.heading: Russia Turns to India for Gasoline as Ukraine's Refinery Strikes Bite
sections.body:
In a striking reversal for one of the world's largest energy exporters, Russia has begun importing gasoline from India to cover domestic fuel shortages caused by Ukrainian drone attacks on its refineries. According to Reuters, at least 60,000 metric tonnes of petrol have been dispatched from India, with two tankers carrying 30,000-40,000 tonnes each currently en route to Russian ports. Russia is seeking to import 400,000 tonnes monthly from India and Belarus combined.

President Putin publicly acknowledged the fuel shortage for the first time on June 29, calling it a 'temporary deficit' and pledging to expedite refinery repairs and increase imports. Russia's parliament approved tax code amendments to subsidize fuel imports. CNBC reports this marked the first time Putin detailed the extent of Ukraine's deep-strike success against Russian energy infrastructure. The Institute for the Study of War (ISW) said Putin's comments acknowledged the effects of Ukraine's campaign but sought to 'promote a facade of stability.'

Ukraine has intensified its long-range drone campaign, striking the Moscow refinery (Gazprom's largest fuel supplier to the Moscow region) and the Ufa refinery — one of Russia's largest lubricant producers, hit twice in one week, according to President Zelenskyy. Fights have broken out at Russian gas stations, and gasoline prices have hit records.

The irony is structural: India is buying discounted Russian crude — Russian oil accounted for more than half of India's total crude imports in June — and now selling refined product back to Russia. It's a supply chain running in both directions through the same partner.
sections.whyItMatters: A net energy exporter is importing gasoline. Ukraine's refinery strikes have created a real domestic fuel crisis in Russia, and the workaround — buying refined product from the same country buying your discounted crude — exposes the limits of wartime self-sufficiency.

sections.heading: UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker; France Follows
sections.body:
Royal Marine Commandos boarded the Russian shadow fleet tanker Smyrtos in a six-hour operation in the English Channel on June 15 — the first-ever UK military seizure of a sanctioned vessel. The tanker, carrying 98,000 tonnes of crude oil, had sailed from Russia's Ust-Luga port. An Indian national, Captain Ajay Pant, 38, was arrested and charged with contravening UK Russia sanctions, facing up to 10 years in prison. His case was sent to Bournemouth Crown Court with a hearing set for July 16.

The operation, involving Royal Marines, the National Crime Agency, and RAF support, was weeks in planning. Prime Minister Keir Starmer called it 'yet another blow to Russia.' Defence Secretary Dan Jarvis told Parliament the UK has sanctioned over 550 shadow fleet vessels and that nearly 200 have been forced to anchor due to UK and allied actions.

Three days later, a sanctioned Russian-flagged tanker, the Forwarder, entered the English Channel — the first shadow fleet vessel to do so since the Smyrtos seizure. The Royal Navy patrol vessel HMS Tyne was observed operating near it. Ship-tracking data showed other sanctioned vessels rerouting to avoid the Channel after the seizure.

French commandos have since stormed another Russian shadow fleet tanker, according to The Sun, expanding the interdiction campaign beyond the UK.

Russia's shadow fleet of over 700 vessels carries an estimated 75% of Russia's sanctioned oil exports, per the UK Ministry of Defence, providing a critical lifeline for funding the war in Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK and France are moving from sanctioning shadow fleet vessels to physically seizing them. The legal precedent — boarding in international waters, arresting the captain, impounding the cargo — marks a significant escalation in sanctions enforcement. Watch whether this deters shadow fleet traffic or simply reroutes it.

sections.heading: G7 Launches Critical Minerals Alliance as China Squeezes Supply
sections.body:
G7 leaders at the Evian summit agreed to establish a Critical Minerals Alliance and Crisis Platform with an expanded role for the International Energy Agency, Reuters reports. The initiative includes aligning stockpiling plans, diversifying supply chains, and tracking minerals through global supply chains.

Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi proposed a Joint Stockpiling Cooperation Initiative at the G7 working dinner on June 15, arguing that the Iran-Hormuz crisis 'highlighted the importance of stockpiling critical minerals.' Japan, which already has its own stockpiling system for rare earths, plans to help other G7 members establish similar reserves. Takaichi also called for speeding up diversification of supply sources with like-minded countries and supporting mineral-rich nations.

The move is a direct response to China's export controls on critical minerals. Beijing has been throttling rare earth and mineral exports to Japan as geopolitical pressure, and the G7 alliance is designed to reduce collective dependence on Chinese supply chains. TechRepublic notes the push targets AI, chip, data center, and defense supply chains that face both China-dependency and financing risks.

This is not just a resources play — it's a supply chain architecture shift. The IEA's expanded role in the platform gives the alliance institutional weight beyond a G7 communique, and the stockpiling coordination means members can draw on each other's reserves in a crisis rather than competing for limited supply.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is building the institutional infrastructure to decouple from Chinese critical minerals. Japan's stockpiling expertise gives the alliance a running start. The Hormuz crisis gave it political momentum. But building alternative supply chains takes years — this is a long game announcement, not an immediate fix.

sections.heading: Eurozone Inflation Cools as Energy Shock Fades — But ECB Stays Cautious
sections.body:
Eurozone inflation fell to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May — the first decline since January — as energy prices dropped 1.7% month-on-month, according to Eurostat. The figure came in below the 3.0% consensus forecast. Cooling services inflation also suggested energy costs are not yet passing through significantly to broader price pressures.

The print increases the likelihood the ECB will refrain from raising its key interest rate for a second consecutive meeting at its July session. Pantheon Macroeconomics chief eurozone economist Claus Vistesen said it would take 'a surge in oil prices' for the governing council to hike in July.

But ECB officials are not declaring victory. Chief economist Philip Lane warned about 'second-round effects' from higher energy prices that 'might take a while to show up.' Bundesbank president Joachim Nagel said inflation remains 'excessively high.' Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch suggested one rate hike may suffice, while Madis Kaasik indicated another hike remains likely. ECB officials at the Sintra forum insisted the Iran war inflation shock 'can't yet be judged to be over,' even if hostilities have largely ceased, Bloomberg reported.

Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, speaking at the same Sintra panel, declined to hint at a July rate decision but said inflation is 'too high.' The ECB's next rate decision is due later this month, with fresh macroeconomic forecasts scheduled for September.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy-driven inflation is easing, but the ECB is treating it as a pause, not an all-clear. The concern is second-order effects — higher energy costs working through wages, services, and industrial input prices — that could keep inflation above target even if oil prices stay near $72.

sections.heading: US Covert Ship-to-Ship Oil Operation Moved 90 Million Barrels Out of the Gulf
sections.body:
A Reuters exclusive revealed that the US military oversaw scores of secretive ship-to-ship oil transfers during the Hormuz closure, moving an estimated 90 million barrels of oil — one of the largest emergency maritime logistics operations in the region in recent years. At least 116 vessels participated in the operation since early May, using smaller tankers to ferry crude from Gulf export terminals to larger vessels stationed outside the most vulnerable sections of the strait, often at night, near Sohar in Oman and Fujairah in the UAE.

The tactics mirrored those long associated with Iranian sanctions-evasion operations: staggered sailing schedules, carefully coordinated transfer points, and operations in low-traffic zones. US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said approximately 7 million barrels per day were being moved out of the Gulf with military backing. The operation relied on surveillance aircraft, drones, helicopters, and naval assets for security.

While the covert network helped maintain Gulf exports during the blockade, it is widely viewed as a temporary measure. Reuters notes that shipping companies remain cautious about resuming normal operations, and analysts say it cannot replace the throughput of an open Hormuz. The Indian Express reported that the operation underscores how the disruption forced even the world's most powerful military to adopt the same clandestine logistics that Iran has used for years.
sections.whyItMatters: The US used Iran's own smuggling playbook to keep oil flowing — 90 million barrels via ship-to-ship transfer. The irony is sharp, but the lesson is practical: Hormuz is so critical that even the US Navy had to improvise. The question is whether this playbook remains on standby.

bottomLine: Hormuz is reopening, but the terms are new: Iran is asserting regulatory control and plans to charge fees after a 60-day grace period expires in mid-August. Oil prices have returned to pre-war levels near $72/bbl, but tanker data and freight rates tell a story of continued physical disruption beneath the price calm. Russia's fuel crisis deepened enough for Putin to acknowledge it publicly and for Moscow to start importing gasoline from India — the same country buying its discounted crude. The UK and France are physically seizing Russian shadow fleet vessels, setting enforcement precedents. And the G7 used the Hormuz shock to launch a critical minerals alliance with real institutional backing. The war's energy shock is fading in the headlines, but the structural changes it set in motion — new transit fees at Hormuz, broken OPEC discipline, militarized sanctions enforcement, reshaped supply chains — are just beginning.
sources: Reuters, Saudi Gazette, Al Monitor, BBC, The Hindu, CNBC, Republic World (India), Politico, Indian Express, Mirror, Morningstar/Dow Jones, Bloomberg, Fitch Ratings, Rystad Energy, IEA, ISW, Cyclope, SPI Asset Management, Citi, Morgan Stanley, CFR, Jiji Press, TechRepublic, MarineTraffic, UK Ministry of Defence, NCA, Eurostat, Pantheon Macroeconomics, The Sun
